The Early Years!
site.hclose
site.br
projects.toobig.title: site.hopen
Too Big Matt! Too Big!
site.hclose
projects.toobig.discussion.1: My old Pa was out, working, in the other yard one day, though he was young back then. We had two yards separated by a 6' paling fence. A week or two, earlier, he had mowed the long grass and left it where it fell (we were semi-rural, and more rural than semi). My father was burning some rubbish, I think, in the other yard. I decided that I needed my own fire — out behind the dunny — but, as these things occasionally do when one overestimates one's abilities to control a situation, the fire had gotten away from me.
projects.toobig.discussion.2: My father came around in time to hear me saying, "Too big, Matt! Too big!". That might have been my way of trying to soften the magnitude of my faux pas. Although my brother was younger, he had taught me to light matches without burning myself. Ma soon learnt to hide matches but, alas, by the time I was twelve, I had a dozen different ways to start a fire:-D Now, in later life, I've learnt to control it — mostly — and to great effect.
projects.toobig.discussion.3: This was not the final assault on the old dunny but it was certainly the most spectacular, and the one that came closest to its wooden elements being reduced to ashes.
projects.thefuse.title: site.br
site.hopen
A bang and spark — and a blown fuse!
site.hclose
projects.thefuse.discussion.1: I was still in primary school — Year 6. I had asked Pa if he could purchase a Photovoltaic cell for me. He came home with a light-controlled switch module and mounting for it. It was not what I wanted for the project in question but I set about wiring it up using a 100W incandescent light as a load.
projects.thefuse.discussion.2: The latency of the opto-electromechanical switch provided a useful tool for demonstrating feedback loops. :-D I took my assembly out to Pa, when he arrived home from work, so he could see it. He looked at it — an odd blend of dubious horror and curiosity. He placed it on the floor, switched it on, and was immediately greeted by the same feedback that I had discovered a little earlier.
projects.thefuse.discussion.3: He was not long home from Work. Ma had been out to pick him up from the railway station. Whilst she was out, and before I had wired the light, I decided it would be a good idea to short the active and neutral to a 2 cent piece, no doubt, to see what would happen. I was greeted with a flash, ozone, the smell of burning insulation, and a blown fuse.
projects.thefuse.discussion.4: In panic, I thought, "Awww shit!!!". I ran out to the meter box to inspect the fuse that I expected that I had blown. When I pulled the ceramic block my suspicions were confirmed and impressively so — copper was splattered all over the casing! I found the fuse wire, selected the correct rating and set about repairing the damage. Ma and Pa never knew.
projects.shockingblue.title: site.br
site.hopen
A Shocking Education!
site.hclose
projects.shockingblue.discussion.1: Primary school was a busy time. I possessed something of a cruel streak, though I had never perceived it thus — I liked giving other kids electric shocks. I justified it by not doing unto them what I had not done to myself, a thousand times before. Of course, I did not take into account the element of surprise and betrayal that the poor unfortunates also had to accommodate. It just added to my mirth, I'm almost ashamed to say... The funny thing was that every kid I shocked followed along so that they could watch the next victim. Also surprising was the fact that I could convince one kid after another to hold the two wires. The shock was delivered by the ignition coil from a lawn mower with a couple of D-cell batteries in series.
projects.uniyears.title: site.hopen
University
site.hclose
site.br
projects.undergrad.title: site.hopen
Another Fire
site.hclose
projects.undergrad.discussion.1: I was in my third year, studying Microbiology and Immunology and attending a lab class. We were supposed to be culturing bacteria on agar plates. The two supervising academics were out front and one was yapping incessantly. Students did what student do and started fidgeting. We were supplied with wire loops, for inocculating plates; alcohol, for sterilizing the loop, and a bunsen. The process involved dipping the loop in alcohol, flaming it, dipping it in liquid culture, then inoculating the plate. The alcohol had been supplied in plastic beakers:-| (What could possibly go wrong?). Whilst the lecturer was yapping, one of the students set fire to the alcohol in the beaker, then, sat there with her hand raised whilst the lecturer droned on. Realization was heralded by a melting, deformed beaker! Panic! The lecturer swiped her lab coat past the beaker and spread the fire.
projects.undergrad.discussion.2: It was almost comical. The 2IC did the right thing and covered the minor conflagration with his lab coat (it was closer to hand than a fire blanket) and smothered the fire. Unbelievably, he leaned over the desk and WHOOMP! The hot fumes reignited and singed the 2IC's eyebrows off.
projects.undergrad.discussion.3: Whilst this little melodrama played out, someone at my desk did the same thing. We had a burning beaker. Being as acquainted with fire as I was, I grabbed a piece of paper and placed it between my hand and the beaker, effectively smothering the fire. My fellow student was, I think, grateful for being saved from the same indignity. About two minutes later, the beakers were replaced with glass beakers.
projects.honours.title: site.hopen
Honours — Another Fire
site.hclose
projects.honours.discussion.1: I'd opted to steer away from biology and focus more on chemistry. To that end, I'd pursued an advanced chemistry project that led into my honours year. I was running HPLC separations for a large part of that year. I was consuming litres of hexane/ethyl acetate — or at least, my experiments were. With the volume of solvent I was pumping through the HPLC system, I wanted a larger stock-bottle in which to store my solvent mix. I found one in the store room and it was the grottiest thing I'd seen in awhile. It had some sort of residue that I had been unable to remove with alcohol, acetone, or hot water + detergent. Not even aqua regia would move it.
projects.honours.discussion.2: Chromic acid was the last resort. I decided to make it in situ. My supervisor was working in the lab that day — a rare occurrence, and she had her back to me. I had already loaded two spoons of Chromium Trioxide into the filthy stock bottle and I was in the process of loading a third when I thought I heard a sizzling noise. I stopped, looked to the side of the bottle and I thought I saw a dull orange flash. I took a step back and was rewarded for my efforts with the all too familiar "WHOOMP!!", followed by a rain of green soot, and a fire.
projects.honours.discussion.3: My supervisor heard the noise and turned to see what had happened. In a mildly annoyed but quiet voice, she ordered, "Put that fire out!". I picked the bottle up to swing it to the bench behind me. It was then that she became a little agitated and squawked, "Don't pick it up!". I thought to myself, "If she only knew what I had on my bench, she'd understand!". I ignored her moved the bottle, then covered it with a piece of paper and my hand. She again squawked, "Don't put your hand over it!! Put the stopper in it!!". I thought, "No, there's probably an excess of oxidising agent. If there is any more unreacted reducing agent, the bottle will explode. If, on the other hand, there's an excess of reducing agent, then covering the bottle with my hand is the safest option to avoid an explosion, or a sustained fire that risks shattering the bottle."
projects.honours.discussion.4: What my supervisor was unaware of was that I had about 40 open vials with  about 20-30mL, each, of EtOAc/Hex mixture. I had two large 4L winchester bottles, both open, and I also had an open bottle of diethyl ether. The latter has a flash-point of -40 Dec. C, and it heavier than air. It was a warm day - it could very well have become much, much warmer.
projects.recentyears.title: site.hopen
Recent Years
site.hclose
site.br
projects.x2rebuild.title: site.hopen
An Unexpected Journey: Here there be Dragons
site.hclose
projects.x2rebuild.discussion.1: One evening, at ASD, I set about completing pre-work in preparation for an upgrade of their Cray XT5h supercomputing system — essentially a full backup of the system disks which, together, comprise a Sysset. The computer was a complex beast consisting, in effect, of two machines (an XT5 and an X2 Vector processor) coupled together. As 23:00 approached and my outage window drew to a close, I expected to be bringing the system back online but, instead, found myself having to complete a full system, bare-metal build. What should have been a routine sysset backup went wrong and I ended up with two empty syssets which was inexplicable in itself. (If the old Sysset copy had been inadvertently copied over the working copy, I should have ended up with two out of date copies - I've only just realised that, seventeen years after the fact.)
projects.x2rebuild.discussion.2: I hadn’t worked on that platform before, and the original engineer who had travelled to Chippewa Falls, WI, USA., had left. I later learned that the Cray engineers had built the system over a couple of weeks with our local engineer being trained up at the same time.
projects.x2rebuild.discussion.3: I called the customer — he told me to restore from backup. :-| I explained the circumstances and then started rebuilding from scratch that night. By the next morning I had the XT5 side up. I went home in the morning and left my team completing the localisation of the XT5 half of the system. I returned in the evening and then began rebuilding the vector side — sorting out the broken (it was broken in order to interface the X2; not in the sense of it malfunctioning) folded 3D torus to fat-tree routing, getting a working toolchain together, and rebuilding the runtime environment.
projects.x2rebuild.discussion.4: Within a couple of days the system was stable again, and I went on to complete the upgrade I’d originally planned. A few months later the Cray Engineers contacted me to ask how I’d done it, because they’d had to roll their own upgrade back. At that time, aside from the training machine at Chippewa Falls, I believe there was only one other XT5h in the world. I'd completed the first successful field upgrade of an XT5h system, and not without some difficulty.
projects.x2rebuild.discussion.5: The main takeaway for me was being comfortable working from first principles when there’s no safety net — just breaking the system down until it becomes tractable.
projects.FPGAarray.title: site.hopen
A Systolic Array by Any Other Name...
site.hclose
projects.FPGAarray.discussion.1: Whilst at ASD I was privileged to have worked on a custom high-density FPGA-based compute platform — a refrigerant-cooled, multi-chassis FPGA compute platform designed as a high-density programmable hardware array. The designers intended it to run a NIOS-2 soft-core processor in an otherwise conventional configuration. One colleague wanted to repurpose the platform as a form of systolic

for a specialised problem set so this required some rework on our side. It wasn’t a standard environment; everything from hardware layout to programming model was specialised. Even as far as hardware programming is concerned, this beast was unique.
projects.FPGAarray.discussion.2: My role involved working with the system at a fairly low level, including learning enough Verilog to interact meaningfully with the hardware side. I wasn’t a hardware engineer by background, but I got to the point where I could develop and test logic, and more importantly, bridge the gap between software and hardware perspectives.
projects.FPGAarray.discussion.3: That included helping bring other engineers up to speed quickly — translating system behaviour, tooling, and constraints into something usable so work could move forward.
projects.FPGAarray.discussion.4: The main takeaway for me was being able to operate effectively in a hardware/software co-design environment, even without deep prior experience, and making complex systems more accessible to others. At risk of bringing the lapsus linguae by a former Prime Minister to mind, I became the repository of domain knowledge concerning that system. Although Cray designed and built the cabinet, power distribution and cooling, they did not, generally, have any involvement with the Motherboards or Daughtercards. The Demarcation line was the backplane. It was, indeed, a privileged role.
projects.diplomat.title: site.hopen
Oh-oh, The Power and the Passion
site.hclose
projects.diplomat.discussion.1: We had an earlier generation of a custom FPGA-based system that had partially failed and fallen out of regular use. A chassis had been taken offline due to a suspected power supply issue, and previous repair attempts hadn’t resolved it.
projects.diplomat.discussion.2: When the system was needed again, I took ownership of diagnosing it. The challenge was that error reporting was minimal, so I had to work empirically—redistributing daughter cards across the system and tracking faults to isolate failing components. The failed power supply — very much not a simple FRU replacement — had already been changed. The fault, however, was in the chassis itself.
projects.diplomat.discussion.3: I re-installed the power-supply into the spare chassis from whence it came, then pulled the failed chassis from the cabinet. I dropped the spare in place and re-wired the physical cabling — high-current 50V DC connections. I was sufficiently aware of my lack of electrical qualifications — and sufficiently frightened of receiving a thoroughly satisfactory whallop from the supply rails — that I spent much of the work with one hand in my pocket. I then worked through replacing the faulty motherboard and multiple daughter cards. This involved redistributing the daughter cards and following the fault to isolate it.
projects.diplomat.discussion.4: By the end of that process, I had the system back to full operational status.
projects.diplomat.discussion.5: The key takeaway for me was structured fault isolation in systems with poor observability — breaking the problem down physically and logically until the failure modes become clear.
